ServiceNow to buy cybersecurity startup Armis for $7.75 billion
data.description:
Dec 23 (Reuters) - ServiceNow has agreed to buy cybersecurity startup Armis for $7.75 billion in cash, the companies said on Tuesday, as the enterprise software maker looks to attract new customers amid growing risks of cyberattacks.

The company is aiming to integrate Armis' security features such as device scanning, threat detection and vulnerability prioritization to its AI-powered platform, a crucial advantage amid increasingly sophisticated cyberattacks that have hit companies ranging from Microsoft to UnitedHealth Group.

ServiceNow shares slumped nearly 12% on December 15 after Bloomberg News reported about the potential Armis deal over the weekend. The selloff wiped off around $20 billion from the company's market value, a sign that investors scrutinized hefty spending goals of smaller cloud players.

In recent months, ServiceNow has splurged on buying security firm Veza, AI company Moveworks and sales automation platform Logik.ai, formerly Logik.io, to expand its customer relationship management footprint and accelerate sales, order management, AI and security capabilities.

Armis, valued at $6.1  billion in a funding round in November, was preparing for an initial public offering.

The startup's latest financing was led by the alternative investment platform of Goldman Sachs. Existing investor CapitalG, Alphabet's VC arm, also participated in the round.

Armis, founded in 2015, unveiled a three-year plan last month to reach $1 billion in annual recurring revenue after crossing the $300 million milestone in August.

ServiceNow closed its $2.85 billion acquisition of Moveworks this month. It spent $506 million on Logik.ai, while financial details for Veza buyout were undisclosed.

Its deal with Armis is expected to close in the second half of 2026.

(Reporting by Kritika Lamba and Jaspreet Singh in Bengaluru; Editing by Sriraj Kalluvila and Shilpi Majumdar)

US core capital goods orders, shipments increase in October
Durable-Goods Orders Swing to Decline in October
data.description:
By Connor Hart


Demand for U.S. durable goods fell in October, following two consecutive monthly increases, according to delayed data published by the Commerce Department on Tuesday.

Total orders for durable goods--which comprise goods meant to last three years or more--declined 2.2% in October, compared with a 0.7% climb in September and a 3% increase in August.

Transportation equipment drove the decline, falling 6.5%. Excluding transportation, new orders ticked up 0.2%, the Commerce Department said.


Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com


(END) Dow Jones Newswires

12-23-25 0855ET

Arif Habib consortium makes highest bid for Pakistan flag carrier at $482 million
data.description:
KARACHI, Dec 23 (Reuters) - A consortium led by investment firm Arif Habib Corp submitted the highest bid for the privatisation of Pakistan's national carrier Pakistan International Airlines in an auction held after initial bids were opened, state television showed on Tuesday.

The consortium submitted a bid of 135 billion rupees ($482.32 million), well above the government's initial minimum price of 100 billion rupees and the base price of 115 billion rupees, at which the auction began.

($1 = 279.9000 Pakistani rupees)

(Reporting by Ariba ShahidWriting by Sakshi DayalEditing by David Goodman)

Orsted sells 55% of Taiwan wind farm to Cathay
data.description:
COPENHAGEN, Dec 23 (Reuters) - Danish offshore wind developer Orsted has agreed to sell a 55% stake in its Greater Changhua 2 offshore wind farm in Taiwan to life insurance company Cathay in a deal worth around 5 billion Danish crowns ($788.74 million), it said on Tuesday.

($1 = 6.3392 Danish crowns)

(Reporting by Stine Jacobsen, editing by Essi Lehto)

Ørsted Sells Majority Stake in Taiwanese Offshore Wind Farm to Cathay
data.description:
Danish energy company Ørsted has signed an agreement with Cathay Life Insurance and Cathay Power to sell a 55 percent stake in the Greater Changhua 2 offshore wind farm in Taiwan, which has a capacity of 632 megawatts. The stake is valued at approximately 5 billion Danish kroner, taking into account existing project financing, according to a press release.

The facility consists of an already operational section and another part currently under construction, which is expected to enter commercial operation in the third quarter of 2026, at which point the transaction is also scheduled to be finalized.

Ørsted will retain responsibility for long-term operations and maintenance, and states that through this deal, the company is moving closer to its goal of securing over 35 billion Danish kroner in divestment proceeds during 2025–2026.

data.title: Cathay to acquire 55% in Ørsted offshore wind farm in Taiwan
